{{wikidata link|Q11563474}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = 湯前神社 |画像 = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
湯前神社の拝殿 (2023年1月撮影) |所在地 = 静岡県熱海市上宿町4-12 | 位置 ={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | 地図 = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=湯前神社|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} |祭神 = 少彦名神 |社格 = 式内社論社・旧村社 |創建 = 天平勝宝元年、または天平宝字年中（8世紀半ば） |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = 2月10日・10月10日 |神事 = 献湯祭（2月10日・10月10日） }} '''湯前神社'''（ゆぜんじんじゃ）は、{{jalink|静岡県|Q131320}}{{jalink|熱海市|Q653364}}に鎮座する{{jalink|神社|Q845945}}である。{{jalink|式内社}}の「久豆弥（くづみ）神社」の{{jalink|論社|Q135022904}}で、{{jalink|旧社格}}は{{jalink|村社|Q134917284}}。社前には{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}までの{{jalink|熱海温泉|Q11569090}}の中心的な源泉だった{{jalink|大湯間歇泉|Q65258433}}があり、この神社の名称・起源もそこに由来している。 現在は近隣の{{jalink|来宮神社|Q11523682}}の{{jalink|兼務社}}であり、{{jalink|御朱印}}も来宮神社で得ることができる。 === 祭神 === {{jalink|少彦名神}}を祀る。少彦名神は温泉の神として{{jalink|日本|Q17}}各地に祀られているが、本来は大湯そのものを神として祀ったものと思われる『式内社調査報告』。。 === 由緒 === 社伝によると、{{jalink|天平勝宝|Q1373169}}元年（{{jalink|749年|Q23624}}）6月、小児に{{jalink|神託|Q217123}}が下り、諸病を除く効果があるので温泉を汲み取って浴せよとの神教があり、その報恩として里人が{{jalink|祠|Q1442984}}を建てて少彦名神を祀ったのに創まるという『特選神名牒』所引「熱海温泉記」。。また{{jalink|天平宝字|Q1346307|天平宝字年中}}（8世紀半ば）に{{jalink|箱根山|Q1067417}}の金剛王院（廃寺{{Refnest|group="注釈"|現{{jalink|箱根町|Q671040|神奈川県箱根町}}にあり、箱根神社の{{jalink|別当寺|Q11396755}}であった。}}）に住した{{jalink|万巻上人||万巻（満願）上人}}が、熱海の海中に温泉が湧いてその熱湯のために多くの魚介類が死んでいたのを哀れみ、海浜に祈祷の壇を築いて100日間の勤行に励むと、満願の日に内陸部へと湯脈が移ったので、その傍らに「湯前権現」と称して温泉の{{jalink|守護神|Q131278957}}として祀るようになったともいう{{jalink|寛文|Q1146228}}7年（1667年）の年紀を持つ「湯前権現拝殿再興勧進帳」（{{jalink|熱海市立図書館|Q28684821}}所蔵）。。 『{{jalink|伊豆国神階帳|Q17193087}}』{{jalink|田方郡|Q1154841}}部に載せる「従四位上 熱海の湯明神」に比定され{{Refnest|group="注釈"|『伊豆国神階帳』は{{jalink|伊豆国|Q860928}}の{{jalink|国内神名帳|Q11420490}}。}}、『{{jalink|元亨釈書|Q4153225}}』に{{jalink|桓舜|Q11537639|釈桓舜}}が説法をしたと伝える「温泉神祠」も当神社の事と推定されているが『静岡県の地名』。、『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』の「久豆弥（くづみ）神社」に当てる説もある{{Refnest|group="注釈"|{{jalink|出口延経|Q108780926}}『{{jalink|神名帳考証|Q11588824}}』等。久豆弥神社の「くづみ」は伊豆国の古代の郷名である「{{jalink|久寝郷||久寝（くすみ）}}」に因むものと見るのが通説であるが、その「くすみ」を伊豆国の隅（「国隅（くすみ）」）と解したり（『{{jalink|日本地理志料}}』）、熱海の温泉湧出を神の業と見、それを「奇霊（くしび）」（{{jalink|奇魂}}）と称したのが「くすみ」と訛ったと解して（竹村茂正『豆州式社考案』）、久寝郷を熱海市に比定するのがその論拠とされる。もっとも久寝郷は現{{jalink|伊東市|Q721163}}市街地一帯を指すとする説が有力なので、久豆弥神社を同市の{{jalink|葛見神社|Q11621015}}に比定する説もある（『式内社調査報告』）。}}。中世以降広く「湯前権現」と称され、{{jalink|鎌倉時代|Q236205}}に{{jalink|源頼朝|Q242800}}を初めとする歴代{{jalink|征夷大将軍|Q131767|将軍}}や{{jalink|鎌倉幕府|Q736839|幕府}}の要人が{{jalink|伊豆山神社|Q611460|走湯}}、{{jalink|箱根神社|Q705310|箱根}}の二所権現に盛んに参詣するようになると、広く湯治の神として喧伝された。 === 祭祀 === ;熱海湯まつり（2月・10月） :毎春秋2季の{{jalink|例大祭}}。 :{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}に{{jalink|江戸城|Q865913}}へ熱海温泉の湯水を献上した古例に因み、秋季例大祭の初日には、「湯汲み道中」が再現する温泉を入れた湯おけを手にパレードが行われる{{Cite news | last = | first = | coauthors = | title = 盛大に「湯くみ道中」 湯前神社例大祭が開幕 | newspaper = {{jalink|伊豆新聞|Q11380576}} | location = | pages = | language = | publisher = 伊豆新聞社 | date = 2013-10-06 | url = | accessdate = }}。 :2日目には、神前に大湯の温泉を献じる「献湯祭」が行われる。 === 境内 === * {{jalink|クスノキ|Q158722}} : 樹幹の二分の一程度が焼損しているが樹勢は旺盛で、樹高：17メートル、幹周り：7.2メートル（熱海市天然記念物指定時）熱海市教育委員会設置、現地案内板による。 * 石鳥居 : {{jalink|安永 (元号)|Q1193910|安永}}9年（1780年）に第7代{{jalink|久留米藩|Q426826}}主・{{jalink|有馬頼徸|Q3135437}}が熱海に来湯したさいに寄進したもの。鳥居両柱の石をくり抜き作られている。高さ：3.45メートル、幅：4.1メートル、直径：0.35メートル。寄進年は鳥居の柱に刻まれている湯前神社奉賛会設置現地案内板による。 * 石燈籠 : 宝暦8年（1758年）に久留米藩主・有馬頼徸が熱海に来湯したさいに寄進したもの。高さ：2.05メートル、柱直径0.35メートル。寄進年は燈籠の柱に刻まれている。 * 手水場 : 手水は源泉が流れていたが、現在は停止している。 * 拝殿・本殿 ファイル:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|石鳥居と天然記念物のクスノキ（左端） ファイル:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|天然記念物のクスノキ ファイル:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|源泉が流れる手水場 === 文化財 === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb| 熱海市指定文化財の石鳥居]] 熱海市指定文化財 ; 建造物 * 石鳥居 1基 - 指定年月日：平成18年12月4日{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html |title=熱海市内文化財一覧 |access-date=2022-06-20 |publisher=熱海市役所教育委員会生涯学習課文化交流室}}。 * 石燈籠 2基 - 指定年月日：平成18年12月4日。 ; 天然記念物 * 湯前神社のクスノキ - 指定年月日：1977年（昭和52年）4月25日。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} === 参考文献 === *教部省撰『{{jalink|特選神名牒|Q11571092}}』（復刻版）、思文閣出版、昭和47年 ISBN 4-901339-07-9（初版は磯部甲陽堂、大正14年） *菱沼勇「久豆彌神社」（『式内社調査報告』第10巻 東海道5、皇學館大學出版部、昭和56年） *『静岡県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系22）、平凡社、2000年ISBN 4-582-49022-0 {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === 外部リンク === *[http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ 湯前神社｜観光・体験｜あたみニュース]（熱海市観光協会） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata 現在は近隣の{{jalink|来宮神社|Q11523682}}の{{jalink|兼務社}}であり、{{jalink|御朱印}}も来宮神社で得ることができる。 === 祭神 === {{jalink|少彦名神}}を祀る。少彦名神は温泉の神として{{jalink|日本|Q17}}各地に祀られているが、本来は大湯そのものを神として祀ったものと思われる『式内社調査報告』。。 === 由緒 === 社伝によると、{{jalink|天平勝宝|Q1373169}}元年（{{jalink|749年|Q23624}}）6月、小児に{{jalink|神託|Q217123}}が下り、諸病を除く効果があるので温泉を汲み取って浴せよとの神教があり、その報恩として里人が{{jalink|祠|Q1442984}}を建てて少彦名神を祀ったのに創まるという『特選神名牒』所引「熱海温泉記」。。また{{jalink|天平宝字|Q1346307|天平宝字年中}}（8世紀半ば）に{{jalink|箱根山|Q1067417}}の金剛王院（廃寺{{Refnest|group="注釈"|現{{jalink|箱根町|Q671040|神奈川県箱根町}}にあり、箱根神社の{{jalink|別当寺|Q11396755}}であった。}}）に住した{{jalink|万巻上人||万巻（満願）上人}}が、熱海の海中に温泉が湧いてその熱湯のために多くの魚介類が死んでいたのを哀れみ、海浜に祈祷の壇を築いて100日間の勤行に励むと、満願の日に内陸部へと湯脈が移ったので、その傍らに「湯前権現」と称して温泉の{{jalink|守護神|Q131278957}}として祀るようになったともいう{{jalink|寛文|Q1146228}}7年（1667年）の年紀を持つ「湯前権現拝殿再興勧進帳」（{{jalink|熱海市立図書館|Q28684821}}所蔵）。。 『{{jalink|伊豆国神階帳|Q17193087}}』{{jalink|田方郡|Q1154841}}部に載せる「従四位上 熱海の湯明神」に比定され{{Refnest|group="注釈"|『伊豆国神階帳』は{{jalink|伊豆国|Q860928}}の{{jalink|国内神名帳|Q11420490}}。}}、『{{jalink|元亨釈書|Q4153225}}』に{{jalink|桓舜|Q11537639|釈桓舜}}が説法をしたと伝える「温泉神祠」も当神社の事と推定されているが『静岡県の地名』。、『{{jalink|延喜式神名帳|Q11064932}}』の「久豆弥（くづみ）神社」に当てる説もある{{Refnest|group="注釈"|{{jalink|出口延経|Q108780926}}『{{jalink|神名帳考証|Q11588824}}』等。久豆弥神社の「くづみ」は伊豆国の古代の郷名である「{{jalink|久寝郷||久寝（くすみ）}}」に因むものと見るのが通説であるが、その「くすみ」を伊豆国の隅（「国隅（くすみ）」）と解したり（『{{jalink|日本地理志料}}』）、熱海の温泉湧出を神の業と見、それを「奇霊（くしび）」（{{jalink|奇魂}}）と称したのが「くすみ」と訛ったと解して（竹村茂正『豆州式社考案』）、久寝郷を熱海市に比定するのがその論拠とされる。もっとも久寝郷は現{{jalink|伊東市|Q721163}}市街地一帯を指すとする説が有力なので、久豆弥神社を同市の{{jalink|葛見神社|Q11621015}}に比定する説もある（『式内社調査報告』）。}}。中世以降広く「湯前権現」と称され、{{jalink|鎌倉時代|Q236205}}に{{jalink|源頼朝|Q242800}}を初めとする歴代{{jalink|征夷大将軍|Q131767|将軍}}や{{jalink|鎌倉幕府|Q736839|幕府}}の要人が{{jalink|伊豆山神社|Q611460|走湯}}、{{jalink|箱根神社|Q705310|箱根}}の二所権現に盛んに参詣するようになると、広く湯治の神として喧伝された。 === 祭祀 === ;熱海湯まつり（2月・10月） :毎春秋2季の{{jalink|例大祭}}。 :{{jalink|江戸時代|Q184963}}に{{jalink|江戸城|Q865913}}へ熱海温泉の湯水を献上した古例に因み、秋季例大祭の初日には、「湯汲み道中」が再現する温泉を入れた湯おけを手にパレードが行われる{{Cite news | last = | first = | coauthors = | title = 盛大に「湯くみ道中」 湯前神社例大祭が開幕 | newspaper = {{jalink|伊豆新聞|Q11380576}} | location = | pages = | language = | publisher = 伊豆新聞社 | date = 2013-10-06 | url = | accessdate = }}。 :2日目には、神前に大湯の温泉を献じる「献湯祭」が行われる。 === 境内 === * {{jalink|クスノキ|Q158722}} : 樹幹の二分の一程度が焼損しているが樹勢は旺盛で、樹高：17メートル、幹周り：7.2メートル（熱海市天然記念物指定時）熱海市教育委員会設置、現地案内板による。 * 石鳥居 : {{jalink|安永 (元号)|Q1193910|安永}}9年（1780年）に第7代{{jalink|久留米藩|Q426826}}主・{{jalink|有馬頼徸|Q3135437}}が熱海に来湯したさいに寄進したもの。鳥居両柱の石をくり抜き作られている。高さ：3.45メートル、幅：4.1メートル、直径：0.35メートル。寄進年は鳥居の柱に刻まれている湯前神社奉賛会設置現地案内板による。 * 石燈籠 : 宝暦8年（1758年）に久留米藩主・有馬頼徸が熱海に来湯したさいに寄進したもの。高さ：2.05メートル、柱直径0.35メートル。寄進年は燈籠の柱に刻まれている。 * 手水場 : 手水は源泉が流れていたが、現在は停止している。 * 拝殿・本殿 ファイル:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|石鳥居と天然記念物のクスノキ（左端） ファイル:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|天然記念物のクスノキ ファイル:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|源泉が流れる手水場 === 文化財 === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb| 熱海市指定文化財の石鳥居]] 熱海市指定文化財 ; 建造物 * 石鳥居 1基 - 指定年月日：平成18年12月4日{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html |title=熱海市内文化財一覧 |access-date=2022-06-20 |publisher=熱海市役所教育委員会生涯学習課文化交流室}}。 * 石燈籠 2基 - 指定年月日：平成18年12月4日。 ; 天然記念物 * 湯前神社のクスノキ - 指定年月日：1977年（昭和52年）4月25日。 === 脚注 === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist}} === 参考文献 === *教部省撰『{{jalink|特選神名牒|Q11571092}}』（復刻版）、思文閣出版、昭和47年 ISBN 4-901339-07-9（初版は磯部甲陽堂、大正14年） *菱沼勇「久豆彌神社」（『式内社調査報告』第10巻 東海道5、皇學館大學出版部、昭和56年） *『静岡県の地名』（日本歴史地名大系22）、平凡社、2000年ISBN 4-582-49022-0 {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === 外部リンク === *[http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ 湯前神社｜観光・体験｜あたみニュース]（熱海市観光協会） {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] {{wikidata link|Q11563474}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}} | status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine; former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Atami|Q653364}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is regarded as a {{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904|candidate shrine}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, and its former rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. In front of the shrine is the {{jalink|Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433}}, which was the principal spring source of {{jalink|Atami Onsen|Q11569090}} until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{jalink|Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{jalink|Japan|Q17}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{jalink|Edo Castle|Q865913}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{jalink|Izu Shimbun|Q11380576}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{jalink|Camphor tree|Q158722}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{jalink|Kurume Domain|Q426826}}, {{jalink|Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

{{wikidata link|Q11563474}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}} | status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine; former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Atami|Q653364}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is regarded as a {{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904|candidate shrine}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, and its former rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. In front of the shrine is the {{jalink|Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433}}, which was the principal spring source of {{jalink|Atami Onsen|Q11569090}} until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{jalink|Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{jalink|Japan|Q17}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{jalink|Edo Castle|Q865913}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{jalink|Izu Shimbun|Q11380576}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{jalink|Camphor tree|Q158722}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{jalink|Kurume Domain|Q426826}}, {{jalink|Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

revision.comment: 1 revision imported: used local AI agents to translate these pages. Contrary to the description it was actually Claude Code for a few, OpenClaw with Codex, and regular Codex. Ollama and DeepSeek were too bad to actually use. see categorization since some of them are very questionable, especially if there was a massive decrease in page size or the {{jawiki| template is minimal or absent on the page

Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}} | status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine; former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Atami|Q653364}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is regarded as a {{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904|candidate shrine}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, and its former rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. In front of the shrine is the {{jalink|Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433}}, which was the principal spring source of {{jalink|Atami Onsen|Q11569090}} until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{jalink|Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{jalink|Japan|Q17}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{jalink|Edo Castle|Q865913}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{jalink|Izu Shimbun|Q11380576}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{jalink|Camphor tree|Q158722}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{jalink|Kurume Domain|Q426826}}, {{jalink|Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. {{wikidata link|Q11563474}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}} | status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine; former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Atami|Q653364}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is regarded as a {{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904|candidate shrine}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, and its former rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. In front of the shrine is the {{jalink|Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433}}, which was the principal spring source of {{jalink|Atami Onsen|Q11569090}} until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{jalink|Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{jalink|Japan|Q17}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{jalink|Edo Castle|Q865913}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{jalink|Izu Shimbun|Q11380576}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{jalink|Camphor tree|Q158722}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{jalink|Kurume Domain|Q426826}}, {{jalink|Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]] [[Category:need translation]]

The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. {{wikidata link|Q11563474}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}} | status = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}} candidate shrine; former {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{jalink|Shinto shrine|Q845945}} in {{jalink|Atami|Q653364}}, {{jalink|Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320}}. It is regarded as a {{jalink|ronsha|Q135022904|candidate shrine}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932}}, and its former rank was {{jalink|village shrine|Q134917284}}. In front of the shrine is the {{jalink|Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433}}, which was the principal spring source of {{jalink|Atami Onsen|Q11569090}} until the {{jalink|Edo period|Q184963}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{jalink|Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{jalink|Sukunahikona|Q849233|Sukunahikona-no-Kami}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{jalink|Japan|Q17}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{jalink|Edo Castle|Q865913}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{jalink|Izu Shimbun|Q11380576}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{jalink|Camphor tree|Q158722}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{jalink|Kurume Domain|Q426826}}, {{jalink|Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{jalink|Edo Castle|Q865913}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{jalink|Izu Shimbun|Q11380576}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{jalink|Camphor tree|Q158722}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{jalink|Kurume Domain|Q426826}}, {{jalink|Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{jalink|Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

{{wikidata link|Q11563474}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}} candidate shrine; former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is regarded as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=candidate shrine}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

{{wikidata link|Q11563474}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=UNKNOWN|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine; former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320}}. It is regarded as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=candidate shrine}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284}}. In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]

Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine; former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is regarded as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=candidate shrine|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433|1=Ōyu Geyser}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090|1=Atami Onsen}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576|1=Izu Shimbun}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826|1=Kurume Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437|1=Arima Yoriyuki}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]]
