Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine; former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is regarded as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=candidate shrine|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433|1=Ōyu Geyser}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090|1=Atami Onsen}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576|1=Izu Shimbun}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826|1=Kurume Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437|1=Arima Yoriyuki}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ゆせんしんしや}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine; former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is regarded as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=candidate shrine|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433|1=Ōyu Geyser}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090|1=Atami Onsen}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576|1=Izu Shimbun}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826|1=Kurume Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437|1=Arima Yoriyuki}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:伊豆国の式内社]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]][[Category:qq]]

In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433|1=Ōyu Geyser}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090|1=Atami Onsen}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576|1=Izu Shimbun}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826|1=Kurume Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437|1=Arima Yoriyuki}}, visited Atami. Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine; former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is regarded as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=candidate shrine|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433|1=Ōyu Geyser}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090|1=Atami Onsen}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576|1=Izu Shimbun}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826|1=Kurume Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437|1=Arima Yoriyuki}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:温泉神社]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:熱海市の建築物]]

The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine; former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is regarded as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=candidate shrine|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433|1=Ōyu Geyser}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090|1=Atami Onsen}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576|1=Izu Shimbun}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826|1=Kurume Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437|1=Arima Yoriyuki}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:静岡県の神社]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings in Atami]]

Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine; former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is regarded as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=candidate shrine|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433|1=Ōyu Geyser}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090|1=Atami Onsen}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576|1=Izu Shimbun}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826|1=Kurume Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437|1=Arima Yoriyuki}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings in Atami]]

Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576|1=Izu Shimbun}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826|1=Kurume Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437|1=Arima Yoriyuki}}, visited Atami. Japanese content == {{Shrine | name = Yuzen Shrine | image = [[File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan05s3.jpg|260px]]
Haiden of Yuzen Shrine (photo taken in January 2023) | location = Kamijuku-cho 4-12, Atami, Shizuoka Prefecture | coordinates = {{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E|region:JP-22_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map = {{Location map|Japan Shizuoka|label=Yuzen Shrine|lat_deg=35|lat_min=5|lat_sec=53.6|lon_deg=139|lon_min=4|lon_sec=16.7|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20|float=center|border=none|position=left|width=200|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|35|5|53.6|N|139|4|16.7|E}}}} | deity = {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}} | status = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Shikinaisha|1=Shikinaisha|WD=Q134917286}} candidate shrine; former {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}} | founded = Tenpyo-shoho 1 (749), or during the Tenpyo-hoji era (mid-8th century) | annual_festival = February 10 and October 10 | rituals = Kento-sai (offering of hot-spring water; February 10 and October 10) }} '''Yuzen Shrine''' (ゆぜんじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in {{ill|lt=Atami|Q653364|WD=Q653364|1=Atami}}, {{ill|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|Q131320|WD=Q131320|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}. It is regarded as a {{ill|lt=ronsha|Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|lt=candidate shrine|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} for "Kuzumi Shrine" listed in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|lt=village shrine|Q134917284|WD=Q134917284|1=Son-sha}}. In front of the shrine is the {{ill|lt=Oyū Intermittent Spring|Q65258433|WD=Q65258433|1=Ōyu Geyser}}, which was the principal spring source of {{ill|lt=Atami Onsen|Q11569090|WD=Q11569090|1=Atami Onsen}} until the {{ill|lt=Edo period|Q184963|WD=Q184963|1=Edo period}}; the shrine's name and origin are associated with this spring. Today it is a branch shrine administered by nearby {{ill|lt=Kinomiya Shrine|Q11523682|WD=Q11523682|1=Kinomiya Shrine}}, and goshuin can be received at Kinomiya Shrine. === Enshrined deity === The shrine enshrines {{ill|lt=Sukunahikona|Q849233|WD=Q849233|lt=Sukunahikona-no-Kami|1=Beauty and the Beast (1991 soundtrack)}}. Although Sukunahikona is worshiped nationwide in {{ill|lt=Japan|Q17|WD=Q17|1=Japan}} as a deity of hot springs, this shrine is thought originally to have worshiped the Oyū spring itself as a deity."Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku." === Origin and history === According to shrine tradition, in June 749 (Tenpyo-shoho 1), a divine oracle was delivered to a child instructing that hot-spring water should be drawn and used for bathing because it would cure illnesses; local residents then built a small shrine and enshrined Sukunahikona in gratitude."Tokusen Jinmeicho," citing "Atami Onsen-ki." Another tradition says that in the mid-8th century, the monk Mangan Shonin, who had resided at Kongooin on Mt. Hakone, pitied the fish and shellfish dying from boiling spring water emerging in Atami's sea. After 100 days of prayer on a ritual platform by the shore, the spring vein shifted inland on the final day, and the deity came to be worshiped there as "Yuzen Gongen," guardian deity of the hot spring."Yuzen Gongen Haiden Saiko Kanjincho" dated 1667 (Atami City Library collection). The shrine is identified with "Atami no Yu Myojin" in the Tagata-gun section of the ''Izu no Kuni Shinkaicho'', and the "Onsen-jinshi" mentioned in the ''Genko Shakusho'' is also inferred to refer to this shrine."Shizuoka-ken no Chimei." There is also a theory identifying it with "Kuzumi Shrine" in the ''Engishiki Jinmyocho''. In the medieval period and later it was widely called "Yuzen Gongen," and as devotion to the two gongen of Izusan (Hashiriyu) and Hakone spread among shoguns and Kamakura shogunate elites from the {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|1=Kamakura period}}, it was broadly promoted as a deity of hot-spring healing. === Rituals === ;Atami Yu Festival (February and October) : The spring and autumn grand festivals. : Based on the old custom of presenting Atami hot-spring water to {{ill|lt=Edo Castle|Q865913|WD=Q865913|1=Edo Castle}} in the Edo period, the first day of the autumn festival includes a "Yukumi-dochu" parade in which participants carry buckets of hot-spring water.{{Cite news | title = "Yukumi-dochu" held in grand style as Yuzen Shrine grand festival opens | newspaper = {{ill|lt=Izu Shimbun|Q11380576|WD=Q11380576|1=Izu Shimbun}} | publisher = Izu Shimbun | date = 2013-10-06 }} : On the second day, the "Kento-sai" is performed, offering hot-spring water from Oyū before the deity. === Precinct === * {{ill|lt=Camphor tree|Q158722|WD=Q158722|1=Camphora officinarum}} : About half of the trunk is fire-damaged, but the tree remains vigorous; height 17 m, trunk circumference 7.2 m (at the time of Atami City natural monument designation).On-site guide board installed by the Atami City Board of Education. * Stone torii : Donated in 1780 (An'ei 9) when the 7th lord of the {{ill|lt=Kurume Domain|Q426826|WD=Q426826|1=Kurume Domain}}, {{ill|lt=Arima Yoritaka|Q3135437|WD=Q3135437|1=Arima Yoriyuki}}, visited Atami. The stone of both pillars is hollowed. Height 3.45 m, width 4.1 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillars.On-site guide board installed by the Yuzen Shrine support association. * Stone lanterns : Donated in 1758 (Horeki 8) by Arima Yoritaka when he visited Atami. Height 2.05 m, pillar diameter 0.35 m. The donation year is engraved on the pillar. * Temizu basin : Spring water formerly flowed here, but it is currently stopped. * Haiden and honden File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan02s3.jpg|Stone torii and the designated camphor tree (left) File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan06s3.jpg|Designated camphor tree File:230127 Yuzen-jinja Atami Japan04s3.jpg|Temizu basin where spring water flowed === Cultural properties === [[File:Torii of Yuzen Shrine.jpg|thumb|Stone torii, designated cultural property of Atami City]] Designated cultural properties of Atami City ;Buildings * Stone torii, 1 structure - designated December 4, 2006.{{Cite web|ja|url=https://www.city.atami.lg.jp/kosodate/bunka/1011168/1001062.html|title=List of cultural properties in Atami City|access-date=2022-06-20|publisher=Atami City Board of Education, Lifelong Learning Division}} * Stone lanterns, 2 structures - designated December 4, 2006. ;Natural monument * Camphor tree of Yuzen Shrine - designated April 25, 1977. === Notes === {{脚注ヘルプ}} === Explanatory notes === {{Reflist|group="注釈"}} === References === {{Reflist}} === Bibliography === * Ministry of Religious Education (ed.), ''{{ill|lt=Tokusen Jinmeicho|Q11571092|WD=Q11571092|1=Tokusen Jinmyōchō}}'' (reprint), Shibunkaku Publishing, 1972. ISBN 4-901339-07-9. * Isamu Hishinuma, "Kuzumi Shrine," in ''Shikinaisha Chosa Hokoku'', Vol. 10 (Tokaido 5), Kogakkan University Press, 1981. * ''Shizuoka-ken no Chimei'' (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 22), Heibonsha, 2000. ISBN 4-582-49022-0. {{Commonscat|Yuzen-jinja}} === External links === * [http://www.ataminews.gr.jp/spot/313/ Yuzen Shrine | Sightseeing and Experiences | Atami News] (Atami Tourism Association) {{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Yuzen Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Shizuoka Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Izu Province]] [[Category:Onsen Shrines]] [[Category:静岡県の旧村社]] [[Category:熱海市の歴史]] [[Category:Buildings and structures in Atami]]
